 Subject: Donald J. Trump
 Knowledge Window: 2025-01-01 → 2025-03-05
 Events in window: 262 | Showing top 8

[2025-01-23] Senate Confirms John Ratcliffe as Trump's CIA Director
The U.S. Senate confirmed John Ratcliffe as the 25th Director of the Central Intelligence Agency (CIA) on Thursday, January 23, 2025, with a 74-25 vote. Vice President JD Vance swore Ratcliffe into office approximately two hours later at the White House.

Ratcliffe previously served as Director of National Intelligence (DNI) during President Trump's first term, becoming the first individual to hold both the DNI and CIA Director positions. He also served as a U.S. congressman from Texas.

During his January 15, 2025, confirmation hearing, Ratcliffe outlined plans for the CIA to be more aggressive in collecting human intelligence and countering U.S. adversaries. Some Democrats, including Senators Ron Wyden and Chuck Schumer, opposed his nomination, citing concerns about partisanship, his experience, and his ability to challenge the administration.
Key facts:
  • 25th Director of the Central Intelligence Agency
  • Confirmation hearing held on January 15, 2025
  • First person to hold both CIA Director and DNI roles
  • First person to serve as both Director of the CIA and Director of National Intelligence
  • John Ratcliffe
  • John Ratcliffe confirmed as 25th CIA Director
  • John Ratcliffe confirmed as CIA Director
  • Pledged to reinvigorate Havana Syndrome investigations
  • Previously served as Director of National Intelligence (DNI)
  • Previously served as Director of National Intelligence during President Trump’s first administration
  • Previously served in the U.S. House of Representatives
  • Ratcliffe previously served as Director of National Intelligence
  • Senate Majority Leader John Thune advocated for speedy vote
  • Senate vote: 74-25
  • Senators Ron Wyden and Chuck Schumer opposed the nomination
  • Senators Tom Cotton and Mark Warner advocated for speedy vote
  • Supports maintaining Section 702 of FISA
  • Vice President JD Vance administered oath
  • Vice President JD Vance administered oath of office
"We will collect intelligence, especially human intelligence, in every corner of the globe no matter how dark or difficult." — John Ratcliffe (Confirmation hearing before the Senate Intelligence Committee)
"We will conduct covert actions at the direction of the president, going places no one else can go and doing things no one else can do." — John Ratcliffe (Confirmation hearing before the Senate Intelligence Committee)
"To the brave CIA officers listening around the world, if all of this sounds like what you signed up for, then buckle up and get ready to make a difference. If it doesn't, then it's time to find a new line of work." — John Ratcliffe (Confirmation hearing before the Senate Intelligence Committee)
"Our world is far too dangerous for any delay in having a Senate-confirmed leader in charge of the CIA." — Senators Tom Cotton and Mark Warner (Statement on Monday after committee advanced Ratcliffe's nomination)
"Under the Biden administration, the intelligence community made some notable misses. Mr. Ratcliffe brings the right experience and the right approach to the CIA." — John Thune (Senate floor, Tuesday, pushing for Ratcliffe's nomination)
"I want the record to show that I strongly oppose the nomination." — Ron Wyden (Senate floor, Tuesday)
"I will respectfully vote no, not because of our political difference, which of course exists, but because I am deeply worried that Mr. Ratcliffe will be unable to stand up to people like Donald Trump and Tulsi Gabbard, who are known to falsify intelligence." — Chuck Schumer (Senate floor, before Thursday's vote)
"it is the privilege of a lifetime to lead the patriotic men and women of the CIA, an agency so vital to America’s national security and one with a culture of unwavering commitment to mission. I’m extremely grateful for the opportunity to serve President Trump again and thank him for entrusting me with this tremendous responsibility." — Director Ratcliffe (Upon being sworn in as CIA Director)
Sources: www.npr.org, www.voanews.com, www.cia.gov (3 sources, verified date)

[2025-03-04] President Trump Delivers 2025 State of the Union Address to Joint Session of Congress
President Donald Trump delivered his 2025 State of the Union address to a joint session of the 119th United States Congress on March 4, 2025. The address, his first of a second non-consecutive term, began at 9:19 p.m. EST in the House chamber of the U.S. Capitol and lasted 1 hour and 39 minutes. Vice President J.D. Vance and Speaker of the House Mike Johnson presided. Trump outlined proposals including border security measures, economic plans such as extending the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act and eliminating taxes on tips, and enhanced domestic energy production. He also announced plans to withdraw from international agreements including the Paris Climate Accord and the World Health Organization, and orders making English the official U.S. language and banning men from women's sports. The speech drew an estimated 36.6 million viewers. Secretary of Veterans Affairs Doug Collins was the designated survivor. Democratic Representative Al Green was removed and later censured for disrupting the speech.
Key facts:
  • 119th United States Congress
  • 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act
  • 25% tariffs on imported aluminum, copper, lumber, and steel
  • Addressed a joint session of the 119th United States Congress
  • Authorized expansion of oil and gas production ('drill, baby, drill')
  • CHIPS and Science Act (mentioned for repeal)
  • Claimed DOGE identified $1.9 billion waste for 'decarbonization of homes committee' linked to Stacey Abrams
  • Claimed DOGE identified $22 billion waste from HHS for housing/cars for 'illegal aliens'
  • Claimed DOGE identified $45 million waste for DEI scholarships in Burma
  • Claimed DOGE identified $8 million waste for 'making mice transgender'
  • Claimed actions in 43 days: Nearly 100 Executive Orders, over 400 Executive Actions
  • Congressman Sylvester Turner made his last public appearance.
  • Created Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE)
  • Date: March 4, 2025
  • Declared national emergency on southern border
  • Declared national energy emergency

  • Deployed U.S. military and Border Patrol to southern border
  • Designated survivor: Secretary of Veterans Affairs Doug Collins
  • Donald Trump, 47th President of the United States
  • Duration: 1 hour, 39 minutes (longest address to a joint session in at least 61 years)
  • Elon Musk appointed head of DOGE
  • Estimated $500 billion in annual waste, fraud, and abuse in government
  • Estimated 36.6 million television viewers in the U.S.
  • Estimated 36.6 million viewers
  • Event: 2025 Joint Address to Congress
  • Executive order announced to rename Anahuac National Wildlife Refuge to Jocelyn Nungaray National Wildlife Refuge
  • First Lady Melania Trump
  • Four Supreme Court justices attended: Chief Justice Roberts, Associate Justices Elena Kagan, Brett Kavanaugh, and Amy Coney Barrett.
  • Gold Card program for foreign investors contributing $5 million
  • Imposed immediate freeze on all federal hiring
  • Imposed immediate freeze on all foreign aid

  • Presiding officers: House Speaker Mike Johnson, Vice President JD Vance
  • Representative Al Green (D-TX)
  • Representative Tim Walberg (R-MI)
  • Senator Elissa Slotkin (D-MI)
  • Signed executive order to ban men from playing in women's sports
  • Signed order making English the official language of the U.S. (March 2, 2025)
  • Speaker of the House Mike Johnson
  • Speaker: Donald Trump
  • Speech duration: Over 1 hour and 40 minutes
  • Time: 9:19 p.m. EST to 10:59 p.m. EST
  • Trump's claim on immigration: "21 million people poured into the United States" during Biden administration. Fact-checkers reported 10.4, 10.8, or 14 million apprehended. Congressional Republicans estimated at least 2 million unapprehended.
  • US aid to Ukraine: Congressional records show ~$175 billion; Kiel Institute figures: US allocated $121 billion + $5 billion committed; European countries contributed ~$140 billion + $122 billion committed.
  • United Nations Human Rights Council (mentioned for withdrawal)
  • United States Capitol
  • Venue: House Chamber, U.S. Capitol, Washington, D.C.
  • Vice President J.D. Vance
  • Withdrew from Paris Climate Accord
  • Withdrew from U.N. Human Rights Council
  • Withdrew from World Health Organization (WHO)
  • World Health Organization (mentioned for withdrawal)
"America is back." — Donald Trump (Opening of the 2025 State of the Union address)
"President Trump's message of making America secure and competitive again are welcome words for the people of Michigan." — Representative Tim Walberg (R-MI) (Post-speech reaction to the 2025 State of the Union address)
"We have accomplished more in 43 days than most administrations accomplished in 4 years, 8 years — and we are just getting started." — Donald Trump (2025 Joint Address to Congress)
"Within hours of taking the oath of office, I declared a national emergency on our southern border, and I deployed the U.S. military and Border Patrol to repel the invasion of our country, and what a job they've done." — Donald Trump (2025 Joint Address to Congress)
"And two days ago I signed an order making English the official language of the United States of America." — Donald Trump (2025 Joint Address to Congress)
"I also signed an executive order to ban men from playing in women's sports." — Donald Trump (2025 Joint Address to Congress)
"And to that end I have created the brand new Department of Government Efficiency. DOGE! Perhaps you've heard of it, perhaps. Which is headed by Elon Musk, who is in the gallery tonight." — Donald Trump (2025 Joint Address to Congress)
"Do you want to keep it going for another five years?" — Donald Trump (Questioning continued U.S. funding for Ukraine during his speech)
"Yeah, yeah, you would say, 'Pocahontas says yes.'" — Donald Trump (Referring to Senator Elizabeth Warren during discussion of Ukraine funding)
"I don't know why we can't fully celebrate [...] I think that's something we could all celebrate." — John Fetterman (Fox News interview on March 6, regarding the DJ Daniel incident during the speech)
"quite insulting" — Lejone Mpotjoane (Criticizing Trump's remark referring to Lesotho as a nation 'which nobody has ever heard of')
Sources: grokipedia.com, www.cbsnews.com, en.wikipedia.org (3 sources, verified date)

[2025-02-28] Trump and Zelenskyy Clash Over Ukraine War Strategy and US Aid in Contentious Oval Office Meeting
U.S. President Donald Trump, Vice President JD Vance, and Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy met in the Oval Office on February 28, 2025. The meeting addressed continued U.S. support for Ukraine and a potential mineral resources agreement. It concluded without a clear resolution. During the discussion, Trump and Vance called for gratitude for U.S. aid and urged diplomacy and an immediate ceasefire. Zelenskyy cited Russia's history of breaking agreements and stated the need for security guarantees. Trump stated Ukraine was not winning the war and required U.S. support for leverage, adding that Russian President Vladimir Putin respected him. Trump told Zelenskyy that Ukraine must reach a deal or face a withdrawal of U.S. support. Following the meeting, the Trump administration suspended intelligence and military aid to Ukraine for approximately one week. Aid resumed after Zelenskyy agreed to an unconditional 30-day ceasefire, which Russia subsequently rejected. U.S. officials, including Secretary of State Marco Rubio, appeared on news shows on March 2, 2025, to support Trump's "America first" approach. European leaders, including European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen and UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer, gathered in London on March 2, 2025, to express unified support for Ukraine.
Key facts:
  • Ceasefire signed in 2019 by Zelenskyy, Macron, and Merkel
  • Date: February 28, 2025
  • European leaders present at London summit: European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen, Finnish President Alexander Stubb, France's President Emmanuel Macron, UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky, Romania's Interim President Ilie Bolojan, European Council President Antonio Costa, Canadian Prime Minister Justin Trudeau
  • European leaders' summit held in London on March 2, 2025
  • Intended agreement: Ukraine–United States Mineral Resources Agreement
  • Location: Oval Office

"For four years, the United States of America, we had a president (Joe Biden) who stood up at press conferences and talked tough about Vladimir Putin, and then Putin invaded Ukraine and destroyed a significant chunk of the country." — JD Vance (Opening statement on diplomacy and past US policy)
"So he occupied it in 2014. So during a lot of years — I’m not speaking about just Biden, but those times was Obama, President (Barack) Obama, then President Trump, then President Biden, now President Trump, and God bless, now, President Trump will stop him — but during 2014, nobody stopped him." — Volodymyr Zelenskyy (Responding to Vance's call for diplomacy and historical context of Russian aggression)
"Mr. President, with respect, I think it’s disrespectful for you to come into the Oval Office to try to litigate this in front of the American media. Right now, you guys are going around and forcing conscripts to the front lines because you have manpower problems. You should be thanking the president for trying to bring an end to this conflict." — JD Vance (Criticizing Zelenskyy's demeanor and advocating for gratitude)
"You’re playing cards. You’re gambling with the lives of millions of people. You’re gambling with World War III!" — Donald Trump (Accusing Zelenskyy of recklessness in the conflict)
"You haven’t been alone. We gave you through a stupid president 350 billion dollars. We gave you military equipment. You may have been brave but they used our military equipment." — Donald Trump (Responding to Zelenskyy's claim of being alone in the war)
"But he didn’t break them with me. He wants to make a deal. I don’t know if you can make a deal. The problem is, I’ve empowered you (turning toward Zelenskyy) to be a tough guy, and I don’t think you’d be a tough guy without the United States. And your people are very brave. But you’re either going to make a deal or we’re out. And if we’re out, you’ll fight it out." — Donald Trump (Discussing Putin's trustworthiness and the conditions of continued US support)
"I hope you know that we are all with you and the people of Ukraine for as long as it takes, everyone around this table" — Keir Starmer (Opening remarks at the Sunday summit in London on March 2, 2025)
"We need a leader that can deal with us, eventually deal with the Russians and end this war" — Mike Waltz (Appearance on CNN’s “State of the Union” on March 2, 2025)
"And if it becomes apparent that President Zelensky’s either personal motivations or political motivations are divergent from ending the fighting in this country, then I think we have a real issue on our hands" — Mike Waltz (Appearance on CNN’s “State of the Union” on March 2, 2025)
"Either he needs to come to his senses and come back to the table in gratitude, or someone else needs to lead the country to do that" — Mike Johnson (Appearance on NBC’s “Meet the Press” on March 2, 2025)
"the shaking of the head, the kind of — the arms crossed, we just — we found it incredibly disrespectful" — Mike Waltz (Sunday statement on March 2, 2025, regarding Zelensky’s actions during the Friday meeting)
"found every opportunity to try to ‘Ukraine-splain’ on every issue" — Marco Rubio (Appearance on ABC News’ “This Week” on March 2, 2025)
"He’s the peacemaker, and if Zelensky is not interested in peace, then he should go home and think about it" — Howard Lutnick (Appearance on Fox News’ “Sunday Morning Futures” on March 2, 2025)
"putting America first" — Mike Johnson (Remarks highlighted in a White House email sent to reporters on March 1, 2025, praising Trump)
"unwavering leadership" — Tulsi Gabbard (Remarks highlighted in a White House email sent to reporters on March 1, 2025, thanking Trump)
"the age of ‘America last’ foreign policy is over" — Sen. Eric Schmitt (Remarks highlighted in a White House email sent to reporters on March 1, 2025)
"The plan worked, but now Zelensky has to follow through, and the fact that he acted as he did, I think, was a great disappointment" — Mike Johnson (Statement on March 2, 2025, regarding Zelensky's actions during the meeting)
"inappropriate" — Mike Turner (Statement on CBS’ “Face the Nation” on March 2, 2025, describing Zelensky’s actions during the meeting)
"You got to hear President Trump. I mean … he engaged by saying, you know, people are dying. … We need to stop the dying. … Let’s go forward with the peace negotiations, and then we’ll determine what security arrangements need to be made around the peace negotiations" — Mike Turner (quoting Trump) (Appearance on CBS’ “Face the Nation” on March 2, 2025)
Sources: en.wikisource.org, en.wikipedia.org, us.cnn.com (3 sources, verified date)

[2025-01-25] US Senate Confirms Kristi Noem as Secretary of Homeland Security
The U.S. Senate confirmed South Dakota Governor Kristi Noem as the 8th Secretary of Homeland Security on January 25, 2025, by a 59-34 vote. Noem, who previously served as South Dakota's 33rd Governor and its first female governor, resigned following her confirmation. Lt. Gov. Larry Rhoden succeeded her.

Noem is the fourth confirmed member of President Trump's cabinet, following Secretary of State Marco Rubio, CIA Director John Ratcliffe, and Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth. Her nomination advanced from the Senate Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs Committee with a 13-2 vote.

As Secretary, Noem will oversee the Department of Homeland Security. She has expressed support for reinstating the 'Remain in Mexico' policy, shutting down the CPB One App, and ending birthright citizenship. Noem also identified cybersecurity, disaster relief, and counterterrorism as critical DHS missions. The department's responsibilities include border security measures, visa changes, and deportation efforts.
Key facts:
  • 7 Democrats voted for confirmation
  • 8th Secretary of the Department of Homeland Security
  • Acting Secretary Benjamine Huffman
  • Border czar Tom Homan

  • Former Governor of South Dakota
  • Fourth of President Donald Trump's nominees confirmed
  • Kristi Noem confirmed as Secretary of Department of Homeland Security (DHS)
  • Kristi Noem confirmed as Secretary of Homeland Security
  • Lt. Gov. Larry Rhoden succeeded Noem as South Dakota governor
  • Noem is the fourth confirmed member of Trump's cabinet
  • Noem resigned as South Dakota governor

  • Noem supports reinstating 'Remain in Mexico' policy (Migrant Protection Protocols)
  • Noem supports shutting down the CPB One App

  • Senate confirmation vote: 59–34
  • Senate vote: 59-34
  • Stephen Miller is deputy chief of staff and a key architect of the president's immigration agenda
  • Tom Homan picked as 'border czar'
"The reason that I asked for it is because I knew it was the president's number one priority." — Kristi Noem (Senate Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs Committee confirmation hearing)
"I knew that it would be needed to have someone in the position, that would do what the president promised the American people and would be strong enough to do it." — Kristi Noem (Senate Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs Committee confirmation hearing)
"We must be vigilant and proactive and innovative to protect the homeland. The challenges in front of us are extremely significant, and we must secure our borders against illegal trafficking and immigration. We must safeguard our critical infrastructure to make sure that we're protected against cyber attacks, respond to natural disasters and also terrorism." — Kristi Noem (Confirmation hearing)
"As a nation, we have the right and the responsibility to secure our borders against those who would do us harm. And we must create a fair and lawful immigration system that is efficient and is effective, and that reflects our values." — Kristi Noem (Confirmation hearing)
"work very well together and talk and communicate all the time. And we'll be working together on a daily basis when we're in our positions under the new administration. And I would say there's no authority being planned to be taken away from the department or myself if I'm in the role." — Kristi Noem (Confirmation hearing, regarding working with Tom Homan)
"As the Secretary of the Department of Homeland Security, I will work every day to keep all Americans safe and secure. One of my top priorities is achieving President Trump’s mandate from the American people to secure our southern border and fix our broken immigration system. The Trump Administration will once-again empower our brave men and women in law enforcement to do their jobs and remove criminal aliens and illegal gangs from our country. We will fully equip our intelligence and law enforcement to detect and prevent terror threats and will deliver rapid assistance and disaster relief to Americans in crisis. I thank President Trump and the US Senate for their trust in me. Together, we will ensure that the United States, once again, is a beacon of freedom, safety, and security for generations to come." — Kristi Noem (Statement on her confirmation as Secretary of Homeland Security)
Sources: www.npr.org, www.foxnews.com, www.dhs.gov (3 sources, verified date)

[2025-01-20] Donald Trump Signs Record 26 Executive Orders on First Day of Second Presidency
Donald Trump began his second term as President of the United States on January 20, 2025, signing 26 executive orders. On that day, he declared a "national energy emergency" and initiated the United States' withdrawal from the 2015 Paris Climate Agreement. He signed Executive Order 14154, "Unleashing American Energy," directing federal agencies to revise regulations on domestic energy resources and revoking the American Climate Corps.

Other initial actions included initiating withdrawal from the World Health Organization, revising federal recognition of gender identity, establishing the Department of Government Efficiency, pardoning over 1,500 individuals involved in the January 6 Capitol riot, designating Mexican drug cartels as foreign terrorist organizations, and declaring a national emergency at the southern border.

The administration also announced cabinet and cabinet-level nominations, including Marco Rubio for Secretary of State and Pamela Bondi for Attorney General. Individuals with backgrounds as oil, gas, and chemical lobbyists were appointed to the Environmental Protection Agency. Some of these orders have been blocked in court.
Key facts:

[2025-01-20] Donald Trump Inaugurated as 47th President, Signs Record 26 Executive Orders on First Day
Donald Trump was inaugurated as the 47th President of the United States on January 20, 2025, in the Capitol Rotunda, marking the start of his second non-consecutive term. JD Vance was simultaneously inaugurated as the 50th Vice President. On his first day in office, President Trump signed 26 executive orders and issued 12 memorandums. These initial actions included prohibiting the weaponization of federal agencies, repealing previous executive actions, banning federal censorship, ending the electric vehicle mandate, and initiating the second withdrawal process from the World Health Organization and the Paris Climate Accords.

Also on January 20, Susie Wiles assumed office as the 32nd White House Chief of Staff, becoming the first woman to hold the position. She succeeded Jeff Zients. President Trump also designated individuals to serve in acting capacities for various cabinet and cabinet-level positions, including Gary Washington as acting Secretary of Agriculture and Lisa Kenna as acting Secretary of State, pursuant to U.S. laws. The Republican Party retained its legislative majority in the House and secured the Senate majority in the 2024 elections.

[2025-01-20] Trump Issues Executive Orders for Federal Employee Hiring Freeze and Return to In-Person Work
President Trump, following his inauguration on January 20, 2025, issued a series of executive actions. He directed the Secretary of State to align U.S. foreign policy with an "America First" directive, prioritizing American interests and citizens. On immigration, actions included "realigning" the Refugee Admission Program, designating cartels as foreign terrorist organizations, declaring a national emergency at the southern border, and directing construction of a border wall. The administration also moved to end "catch and release," reinstate "Remain in Mexico," and suspend refugee resettlement from January 27.

Regarding federal employees, Trump mandated all executive branch personnel return to in-person, full-time work, ending remote arrangements. He also imposed a 90-day hiring freeze on federal civilian employees, effective January 20, 2025, for unfilled positions, with exemptions for national security and immigration enforcement. The freeze excludes military personnel and prohibits using third-party contractors to bypass it. Additionally, Trump initiated actions to withdraw the U.S. from the Paris Agreement, declared a national energy emergency, and temporarily halted new wind energy projects on federal lands and waters. These orders also paused funding from the Inflation Reduction Act and Bipartisan Infrastructure Law.
Key facts:
  • "Realigning" the Refugee Admission Program
  • Agencies involved in workforce reduction plan: Office of Management and Budget (OMB), Office of Personnel Management (OPM), United States DOGE Service (USDS)
  • America First Policy Directive
  • Declaring a national emergency at the southern border
  • Designating cartels as foreign terrorist organizations
  • Directing Attorney General to seek capital punishment for murder of law enforcement officers and capital crimes by those without legal status
  • Ending "catch and release" policy
  • Ending asylum and closing the border to those without legal status via proclamation
  • Ending automatic citizenship for children born in the U.S. to parents without legal status
  • Executive actions signed on January 20, 2025
  • Executive orders pause funding from Bipartisan Infrastructure Law
  • Executive orders pause funding from Inflation Reduction Act
  • Exemptions for hiring freeze: military personnel, immigration enforcement, national security, public safety positions, legally required positions, OPM-granted exemptions
  • Hiring freeze duration: 90 days (general), indefinite for IRS
  • Hiring freeze effective for positions unfilled as of 12:00 p.m. on January 20, 2025
  • Hiring freeze for federal civilian employees in the executive branch
  • IRS subject to freeze until lifted by Secretary of the Treasury, OMB, and USDS
  • Inflation Reduction Act directed hundreds of billions of dollars to boost renewable energy, electric cars, and cleaner manufacturing
  • Inflation Reduction Act signed in 2022
  • Issued to the Secretary of State
  • Large-scale deportation raids to begin as soon as January 21, 2025
  • Mandates alignment of Department of State policies with 'America First' foreign policy
  • National energy emergency declared
  • President Trump's inauguration: January 20, 2025
  • Reinstating "Remain in Mexico" policy
  • Return-to-work order for executive branch employees
  • Signed by the President
  • Suspending U.S. Refugee Admissions Program from January 27
  • Temporary halt on new wind energy projects on federal land and waters
  • Tom Homan named as incoming Trump border czar

[2025-01-29] Lee M. Zeldin Sworn in as 17th EPA Administrator
Lee M. Zeldin was sworn in as the 17th Administrator of the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) on January 29, 2025. The Senate confirmed Zeldin, a former Republican congressman from New York, by a vote of 56-42. Three Democrats, Sens. Ruben Gallego of Arizona, Mark Kelly of Arizona, and John Fetterman of Pennsylvania, joined 53 Republicans in supporting his confirmation.

During his January 16 confirmation hearing, Zeldin stated he would uphold the EPA's mission to protect human health and the environment and acknowledged the basic science of climate change. He also pledged to support EPA career staff and expressed a moral responsibility to be an environmental steward. Zeldin's confirmation aligns with President Trump's stated goal of revising environmental regulations, including a climate rule aimed at tailpipe emissions. Zeldin previously served on Trump's defense team during his first impeachment and has a 14% lifetime environmental score from the League of Conservation Voters.
Key facts:
  • 17th Administrator of the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)
  • 3 Democrats supported: Sens. Ruben Gallego, Mark Kelly, John Fetterman
  • All 53 Republicans supported
  • Became youngest attorney in New York State at 23
  • Climate rule aims to cut tailpipe emissions of greenhouse gases
  • Climate rule compels automakers to increase sales of hybrid and all-electric vehicles

  • Deployed to Iraq in 2006
  • Expected to dismantle major environmental regulations
  • Expected to dismantle the largest climate rule ever enacted by the federal government

We will maintain and expand the gold standard of environmental stewardship and conservation that President Trump set forth in his first administration while also prioritizing economic prosperity. I look forward to working with the agency’s talented staff and scientists to deliver results for the American people.
